text: The surgeon incises the peritoneal reflection overlying the bladder, exposing the space of Retzius.
The surgeon visualizes the dorsal vein complex with visible blood flow through the vein. The endopelvic fascia is incised, and the dorsal venous complex is skeletonized before being suture ligated.
Santorini's plexus is ligated to prevent back bleeding, followed by an incision made in the bladder neck.
A sound is placed through the incision to guide further dissection of the bladder neck. The surgeon inspects the open bladder and proceeds to incise the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon continues the dissection until the vas deferens and seminal vesicles are identified. The vas deferens is then divided, and the seminal vesicle is dissected free to be incorporated into the final specimen.

The surgeon retracts the vas deferens and seminal vesicles to expose the posterior Denonvilliers' fascia, which is incised to develop a plane between the rectum and prostate. The vascular pedicles of the prostate are dissected and occluded using Weck clips.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to dissect and separate the neurovascular bundle from the prostate, aiming to preserve erectile function. The magnification provided by the robotic system enhances the precision of the dissection and preservation of the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the apex of the prostate, ensuring the neurovascular bundle remains completely intact and preserved.

The surgeon divides the urethra, exposing the Foley capillary vein. The Foley catheter is retracted, and the posterior urethra is divided. The recto-urethralis is severed, freeing the prostate and completing the prostatectomy.
The surgeon excises the lymph node packet to evaluate for metastatic disease, revealing the intact obturator nerve within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a vesicourethral anastomosis using a running suture, with the robotic system providing precise control for the suturing process. The anastomosis is completed under direct vision.
The surgeon uses a double arm suture to create a watertight seal for the vesicourethral anastomosis. The suture is tied, and the bladder is inflated, showing no leakage.
The surgeon completes the prostatectomy, extracting the specimen for pathological examination. The surgical field shows negative margins, indicating no visible residual cancerous tissue.
